turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We will hear arguments next in 72-1660, Blackledge against Perry. Mr. League, you may proceed.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice and may it please the Court. I am Dick League from Raleigh, North Carolina to argue this case for the petitioners. Before I begin an argument, I?d like to apologize having each of you for the shabby condition, I understand the briefs and records that came up before you. I hope you accept my apologies in that regard and that it won?t let you change your view of the case too bad. This case presents two issues to the Court for decision. First, whether or not double jeopardy is a matter that would be waived by a voluntary and intelligent plea of guilty and, secondly, if the record in this case is good enough to show that such plea was made here once voluntarily and intelligently. The facts on which this arises are as briefly as follows. Jimmy Seth Perry was tried in the lower level of our two-tier system in North Carolina, the District Court, on a warrant charging him with misdemeanor assault. After conviction there and sentenced to a consecutive six months sentence, he appealed to the Superior Court division, the higher division of the two-tier system in which there is a trial by jury. He appealed for trial de novo as a matter of right. Prior to the case coming on to be called later, the Solicitor obtained an indictment charging him with a higher grade of the offense, felony assault. And to this, he pled and received a sentence in the Superior Court of some five to seven years, however, this sentence was consecutive to the sentence he had. At the time he made the plea and, accordingly, the actual extension of time beyond the six months consecutive sentence he received below something like about three-and-a half months at that time. Subsequently, in this proceeding in the Eastern District, he obtained habeas corpus relief on the issue of pretrial credit --

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Mr. League, could you speak up a little bit.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: I?m sorry, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: I?m having a little bit of difficulty hearing you.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: I?m definitely sorry. Right. I tend to say and point in the facts as I had just mentioned, Your Honor, was that the five to seven-year sentence concurrent with the sentence he was then serving operated to giving him about a three-and-a half month increase over the six-month consecutive sentence he?d received in District Court, until the time of this action when he received pretrial credit on the first penalty which created a greater disparity between the two. In my position, briefly, on the first issue is that double jeopardy is a matter which would be waived as an independent basis of collateral attack by virtue of his guilty plea in this case. And he can only use that in a habeas corpus action as evidence of some other basis for relief such as, in effect, of the assistance of counsel. Mr. Keenan has suggested to you that you ought to adopt a system-related, guilt-related distinction with regard to determining whether or not such waiver has been made in any given case. But I argue that you should not accept that, and it?s not always clear just what is guilt and what is system-related. In fact, part of the rationale, as I understand it, of the double jeopardy clause is to prevent punishment, the conviction of the innocent through repeated prosecutions. It?s according that, I think, this right would definitely partake both being a system-related right and a right which is guilt-related as well.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Which stage do you say innocence is involved in double jeopardy?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: To prevent one of the basis for the prohibition against that, Your Honor, is to prevent the likelihood of the conviction of the innocent through repeated prosecutions. So, in that sense, I would view it as possibly guilt.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Do you mind giving a citation for that?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: A citation for that?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: I believe it is in Benton v. Maryland. I believe it?s in Green.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: That innocence is a part of it?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Just what I said, Your Honor, that one of the rational -- one of the parts of the rationale against double jeopardy is what I have said.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Double jeopardy is against being twice tried.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Yes, sir, but I?m going beyond what it says.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: What does the innocence got to do --

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: The statute --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: You want to add that to it?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: You want to add that to it?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: No, sir. I believe it has been stated in those decisions that part of the rationale underlying the prohibition on double jeopardy is a possibility of conviction of the innocent through repeated prosecutions. Now, that?s not the only part prior to that, I believe, in the quotation. It?s something like relief of anxiety, embarrassment, and ordeal. The state with all its resources should not be permitted multiple attempts. All these things are mentioned there and I do recall that they are in both the Benton and the Green decisions. Perhaps I?m wrong on the latter, but I believe I?m right on the former.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Mr. League.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Yes, sir?

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Keep your voice up as much as possible.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: I?m sorry, Your Honor.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We?re all having a little difficulty.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: I am terribly sorry. In addition, as a second reason, I would urge upon you for rejecting the system and guilt-related distinction as the basis for waiver is that as you look at the things that are normally not waived by a guilty plea such things as mental incompetence, coercion, ineffective assistance, failure to plea, you see that while they are, in some sense, system-related they also have a common factor which isn?t present in this case. And I think that common factor is that in none of those cases is a plea, a reliable indicator that there may be a valid basis for it. Certainly in Perry?s case, if we are just to throw aside his accusations or his allegations, pardon me --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is the state?s position that there was double jeopardy but that it was waived or why was it even double jeopardy in the first place?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Your Honor, I don?t agree that it was but the thrust of my argument would be, you wouldn?t have to reach that because it would be waived by the plea.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know. It seems to me like that?s a threshold sort of consideration. You don?t need to get to waiver if there were any double jeopardy in the first place.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Well, I understand you avoid the constitutional question if possible so you go to the waiver.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It waives the constitutional question.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Well, I guess so, Your Honor. At initial outlook, it looked less of a constitutional question than double jeopardy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But why would it be a double jeopardy here?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: I don?t think it would be, Your Honor.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: He never was -- he was never tried for the more serious offense but once, was he, and even then he pleaded guilty to it?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: That?s true. I don?t believe in it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well then what was the -- what?s the double jeopardy involved now

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Double jeopardy, according to Judge Larkins, is the raising of the offense. And between the initial trial in the lower court and the subsequent trial --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We have a different offense with different elements.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: That?s why he claim -- Judge Larkins held it was double jeopardy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought double jeopardy was -- it wasn?t double jeopardy if it?s a different crime that he?s tried for a second time.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: I don?t know that, Your Honor. I wouldn?t have thought it was double jeopardy in this case. Though, just by virtue of the states bringing forth the higher degree of the crime for which they could have initially tried him, at least in terms of a probable cause here, at the first level.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would you say there was double jeopardy?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: I say there was not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Not? Well, let?s see if I got this correctly. He was convicted initially on a misdemeanor assault with a deadly weapon, and he appealed that. And, had he been successful on the appeal, he would have had a trial de novo on the misdemeanor charge. Is that right?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: No, sir. He automatically got a trial de novo. Now, the normal expectation would have been that it would?ve been on the misdemeanor charge. But, in the interim, the Solicitor obtained an indictment charging the higher offense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, in other words, when -- he appealed the misdemeanor charge, didn?t he?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And was he tried de novo on the misdemeanor charge?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, because intervening the trial de novo was the indictment on the felony charge?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what?s the difference between the elements of the felony offense and the elements of the misdemeanor offense?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Two additional in this case, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What were they?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: One was inflicting serious injuries and the other intent to kill. To my recollection, they weren?t.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know, but it was the same event, wasn?t it?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: The same event, the same person, the same acts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Except that the -- on the condition of the victim was the same in respect to the trial on the misdemeanor charge as it was in respect to the trial on the felony charge, wasn?t it?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, it?s the only thing that happened. Well, then, why isn?t it double jeopardy?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Your Honor, I don?t think it?s double jeopardy because the thing is no real risk of jeopardy in this lower system unless the man accepts that he can --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He didn?t need any different evidence on the felony charge and you had to -- state had to introduce it on the misdemeanor charge. Well, you had to have some help but since you do not--

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why? I thought you said the condition of the victim was exactly the same.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, whether or not it?s a practical --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Its vindictive then to say but the evidence is required to prove. Well, what different evidence did you have on the felony trial that you didn?t introduce in the misdemeanor charge?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Your Honor, there was no felony trial but we don?t have the record, we don?t have the record of the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: District Court.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Did he ever go to trial on the misdemeanor charge?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: I understand he did from his allegations, Your Honor. The District Court records were gone when we filed our return so we?re --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I?m reading from your brief.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: In the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I?m reading from your brief, and it?s your brief that says he was initially tried and convicted in August 1969 on the misdemeanor assault. He appealed it. And then, you say Perry appealed this assault conviction in the Superior Court and received a trial de novo.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Yes, sir. We pled guilty at the trial de novo, sir.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: And how do you --

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well when you got a -- however, during interim, between appeal and trial de novo, the Solicitor obtained an indictment?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Charging him with a felony?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: That much I know, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And he pleaded guilty to that.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well then I would misread what you say -- and he received a trial de novo? He did not, in fact, receive one. Is that it?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: He received a trial de novo at which he pled guilty. No evidence was put on this time.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was the trial de novo on the misdemeanor charge. That was --

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: That was on the event.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was on the trial of the summary charge.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: That was on the event, Your Honor.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: But have they gone to trial on the higher charge, the second or de novo trial, they would have had to prove intent which was not required in the first case. Is that true?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Intent and serious bodily injury.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: And serious bodily injuries, those two elements.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Right. The proof of what -- it may have been the same in either but it would have to also meet the requirements to sustain those two elements. Yes, sir.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Had he not appealed, could he have been indicted?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: So because he appealed, he was indicted.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: I would say so. Yes, sir.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: (Inaudible).

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: But I would not attach perhaps the same significance to it as Your Honor. This well could have been an event where they tried to get it out of the way down below in the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What would he have been indicted for -- if he hadn?t appealed his misdemeanor conviction?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: I think, Your Honor, under state law at least it would?ve become final within 10 days. It?s only voidable at the instance of the defendant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why could he have not been indicted for it though?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Well, I think because of the greater offense and lesser offense aspect of that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If they have the state law, they won?t?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: In the state law, at least, Your Honor, if you tried for the same act and it would -- the elements of the offense of the lesser one would also be elements of the offense of the greater one --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What?s then?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Then you couldn?t retry.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you have to prove something in addition to prove the point.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Even though that were the case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That?s -- is that state double jeopardy law or is it statutory law or what?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: It would be state law. It well, may well be federal also, Your Honor. But I know it is at least, state.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there any decision of your Supreme Court that supports that proposition?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: What I?ve just said about the lesser included offense?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That it would say that if this misdemeanor judgment had not been appealed, this individual could not have been indicted for the felony?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Well, there?s an old exception. The state cases State v. Burke which Mr. Keenan brings out.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In your brief?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In one of your briefs?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: It?s in his brief. Yes, sir. As an early ?50s case, it relies I don?t know whether directly or the interior sites within rely on one of the Philippine Island cases decided by this case back in the early 1900s. Now, whether or not that?s still a viable exception, I do not know, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It will be double jeopardy, would it not? If a person were tried and convicted for man slaughter, he couldn?t then be indicted for first-degree murder in that court or any other court for precisely the same killing, could he, without violating the Double Jeopardy Clause whether in your state or in other states?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: And that?s what I would I say. Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that correct?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: That?s what I understand. Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That would be my understanding, I think.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: You know, I think if you winded up you?d be able to see on your side over there you can wind it and you will have -- then, we?ll hear you better.

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: I?m sorry for that, Your Honor. My voice has no carriage. I knew that. Returning to why this matter should or should not be waived, I would just say that in reference to these things that we now are not waived there?s no dispute about whether or not they?re not waived. They seem to have at least one common factor which is absent from this case, and that is that the plea is not a reliable indicator that a valid basis for it exists. Here, if we cutout Perry?s allegations that he pled to receive a totally concurrent sentences with his expectation, we see that he traded an argument over double jeopardy for a sentence of about three-and-a half months. So I think, under that basis, it could well be assumed that there was a basis for the plea --

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Does that bring you up against the Pearce case, about increasing sentences in any way?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: No, sir. I don?t think Pearce is applicable to this case by virtue of really what was said in the Colten decision that the possibility for vindictive punishment does not occur sufficiently within the two-tier system to warrant the imposition of the prophylactic rule in Pearce. And in any given case, Your Honor, you could view the case but that?s not the incidence of it to warrant the placing of the Pearce restrictions on this type of situation.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: I suppose some of this comes down to precisely what the word ?offense? means in the Fifth Amendment Double Jeopardy Clause. If you treat offense as being synonymous with criminal acts, you perhaps get one result. If you treat offense as describing and meaning the offense described in the indictment or charge, then perhaps you get another result. Isn?t that true?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Yes, sir, you would.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Which do you think it is?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: In the former.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: The same Act?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Well then, how could you be -- then why haven?t you got a double jeopardy problem?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: I think you don?t get it, Your Honor, because there?s no real risk of punishment inherent in the double jeopardy system. You have to have that risk, and you don?t have it in the lower District Court so long as the plea is voidable by the defendant and -- pardon me, the verdict is voidable by the defendant. That would be my impression of the case. It was also suggested, I believe, that a basis would be, from Judge Larkins? standpoint, was that the reason was there -- pardon me, the right was fundamental. I don?t think much need to be said about that. They?re all that way and I believe this Court rejected that idea recently in the school taxation cases. It?s true that if sustained this plea could bar the prosecution entirely, but that would be the case, I think, with any given constitutional right in a particular case. And lastly, the retroactive decision idea of Mr. Keenan, I had thought, would not aid him if we won on his major contention it wouldn?t be any necessity to come to this and, if he did not, it wouldn?t help. Absent any questions, I?ll rest there and thank you very much for your attention.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Very well. Mr. Keenan.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice and may it please the Court. I appear on behalf of respondent, Jimmy Seth Perry. With the Court?s permission, I would like to run briefly back over the facts because though they are a little bizarre, I nevertheless think that what happened to this case was rather clear. On August 1, 1969, Jimmy Perry was serving a five to seven-year sentence in the North Carolina Department of Corrections for uttering a forged instrument. On that day, a fellow inmate, by the name of Eugene Sawyer received a 14-inch cut in his back. An investigation sued by the authorities of the Autumn Fire Unit in Northampton County, North Carolina. And on the 18th day of August 1969, a warrant was sworn out by a prison guard charging Jimmy Seth Perry with the misdemeanor of assault with a deadly weapon. On August 20, 1969, Mr. Perry was brought to court in the Northampton County District Court which is the proper court in North Carolina for the trial of misdemeanors. All misdemeanors must be tried in the first instance at the District Court level. On that date, the Solicitor or the District Attorney who was trying the case, after conferring with the officer, felt that the conduct necessitated a charge of a felony. Therefore, he made a motion in open court to amend the warrant to charge the felony of assault with a deadly weapon with intent to kill resulting to serious bodily injury. Under North Carolina law, they would then proceed to hold a preliminary hearing in District Court but not in trial. However, later that same day, the Solicitor learnt that he victim, Mr. Sawyer, would refuse to testify against Mr. Perry and, in fact, was claiming totally lack of recall as to what happened. Therefore, he had a very practical problem. He did have a statement that Mr. Perry had made to the officer who had sworn out the warrant that, yes, he had cut him but it had been an accident. Therefore, the Solicitor made that the deliberate choice to move again to re-amend the warrant to charge misdemeanor.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Had the trial court granted the first motion?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes, sir, it had. It had been -- amended the charge a felony and the record of the warrant shows the various markings on it that was first misdemeanor, then a felony, then a misdemeanor again. He proceeded the trial in District Court on a plea of not guilty to the misdemeanor. He was tried before the court alone because, under North Carolina law, one charge with a misdemeanor is not entitled to a jury trial in the District Court. He then, after conviction --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that -- there couldn?t have been a felony charge in that court?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: He could not have been tried for a felony in that court. That court could have conducted a preliminary hearing on the question of --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He could not have been tried there.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: No, he could not have been. He then appealed to the Northampton County Superior Court which is his absolute right under North Carolina law. Upon giving a notice of appeal, the Solicitor then asked the judge if they could go up on the felony. The judge said ?fine,? and found probable cause on a felony warrant which, at that point, didn?t even exist. The state then took an indictment to the Northampton County grand jury --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I think you lost me there. You said the Solicitor asked the judge if he could go up on the felony.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes, this is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, what do you mean by that?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: This was after Mr. Perry had been given a six-month sentence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He?s been convicted of the misdemeanor.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Appealed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And sentenced.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: And sentenced, and appealed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And appealed. And an appeal sets aside that conviction and sentence, does it not, automatically?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes, it does, and it entitles him to a trial de novo.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: In the Superior Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: The Solicitor then inquired with the judge if he could go up on the felony charge.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, what do you mean by ?Judge, can I go up on the felony?? What?s that mean?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: In effect, it means that ?since he?s appealed, I?ve decided I want to try him on the felony rather than the misdemeanor the second time around.?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why did he have to ask the District Judge?s permission?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Well, I would contend that he had no right to ask anybody?s permission because --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: -- because at that point, he was bound by his election to try on the misdemeanor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know that?s your contention, but why do you suppose he thought he had to ask anybody?s permission?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Because, I think he wanted to find a probable cause on the question of a -- on the subject of a felony to take to the grand jury for progression by the grand jury.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Meaning that this is a preliminary hearing, that is it?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did the judge then have a preliminary hearing?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: No, there was no new hearing conducted. The --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What kind of receiving is it?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: It was a farcical proceeding in this particular case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, is there any Provision Court under North Carolina law?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: No, under North Carolina law they could have conducted a trial, which they did conduct on a misdemeanor. They could have conducted a preliminary hearing on a felony.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then bound him over to the Supreme Court.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: And then bound him over to the grand jury. But they did conduct a trial on misdemeanor --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They did both, sort of?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: They only conducted one set of factual here. It?s --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: But in effect, made two judgments. The first judgment was to give him a six-month sentence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They tried him and convicted him on a misdemeanor --

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then they, more or less, had a preliminary hearing and bound him over on the felony. Is that it?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: No new evidence was heard.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No bound, no new evidence. Did they enter -- did the judge enter any kind of order?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes, the judge -- the minutes of that day shows that the judge, on his trial calendar, did enter a notation of a six-month sentence. It also shows that on the official minutes he entered the finding of a probable cause after a plea of not guilty to a misdemeanor and a finding of guilty. Under North Carolina procedure, that is an impossible set of circumstances where he, in effect, was tried on a misdemeanor, pled not guilty, found guilty, then their minutes say probable cause filed. This was filed over to the grand jury. That is just simply not a possible procedure but that is, in fact, what the minutes show.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Do you ordinarily have a preliminary hearing before the solicitor takes the case to the grand jury in North Carolina?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: It?s not required, Your Honor, but it is the normal procedure that a person charged with a felony in the first instance will be brought in the District Court, will be appointed counsel if he is entitled -- if he needs counsel or is entitled to counsel. At that point, a preliminary examination will be conducted. If the judge finds probable cause, it would be filed over to the grand jury. The Solicitor does have the option of going directly to the grand jury.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Mr. Keenan, is any of this in the record?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes, the original petition, the pro se petition of Mr. Perry, basically set forth the facts that he had been tried, given six months --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: You?re not talking about this, are you?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: No, I?m talking about his petition in the record. I?m not talking about the brief.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: But this is not the brief. This is the appendix.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: The appendix just contains excerpts from the petition. I believe the record itself shows that Mr. Perry pled in his petition that he had been tried in District Court.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: I?m talking about all this you said that the man came in and said, and he said and she said, the Solicitor said.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: No, that?s not in the record. That has been gained from my discussion with the counsel involved in the case. The record --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: So, it?s a third-hand.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Second-hand, [Laughter]

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Second-hand, if he is that.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: The record does show that Mr. Perry was tried on a misdemeanor, given a six-month sentence, appealed, and then indicted on a felony. The state, in his answer, admitted these allegations. So, there was no evidentiary hearing conducted in the District Court on these allegations. We contend, well, just to finish up briefly, after the matter had been brought over to the grand jury, he was indicted for the 10-year felony. He came into court. He had a disagreement with his lawyer. A new lawyer was appointed. And at that time, a plea of guilty was entered as charged to the 10-year felony, and he was given a five to seven-year sentence concurrent with the sentence being served at that time which, as the District Court found, effectively raised his sentence by one year, five months and one day over the sentence received in the District Court. Now our basic contention is, first, that the act of the state, in proceeding to charge Perry in attempt to try him with a felony in the Superior Court, denied Perry double jeopardy and that, therefore, there was -- there is an independent constitutional violation to be showed.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Granted in double Jeopardy, don?t you --

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Granted in double Jeopardy.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Granted, right. We contend this because Perry, in appealing the misdemeanor conviction we allege, did not waive his double jeopardy right as to a felony charge with which the state had chosen not to try him in the first instance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Have you have some authority on whether or not federal authority or --

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: We think Green and Pryce control in this particular instance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Green? Well, he was never charged with the -- he was never charged with the felony but he was first tried with misdemeanor.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That is correct, but we think what?s important is that the state had the full opportunity to make a decision whether they were going to proceed on the felony or the misdemeanor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that in Green?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: It?s not Green exactly because --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There?s no case that you could cite.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Well, Wood versus Ross in the Fourth Circuit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it?s not here. We have many cases here.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: No, but I contend that the rationale of --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Green and Rice cannot be distinguished. Excuse me, Green and --

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Green was a case in the federal system, wasn?t it?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Right, and Pryce was a case in the state system which, I think, the Green case was basically applied.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But he was never acquitted. He was neither convicted nor acquitted in the felony.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: That is correct, in the District Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, he was never -- he was tried for the misdemeanor. He was never either acquitted or convicted at the felony.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He couldn?t have been tried for felony.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: He could not have been tried for the felony in the District Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When was he ever tried, convicted, or acquitted of a felony twice?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Our contention is that the state, in a matter like this, has to make an election. They are not free through a series of successive courts to keep elevating the charge up simply by arguing that the lower court did not have jurisdiction to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: To make an election as to what they could have tried him for both in the first instance.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: No, they could not because there is only one incident here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they could have, I suppose, have one -- you think this one was a lesser included offense?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: There is no question under North Carolina law that it is a lesser included offense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I think it could?ve tried him for both of them then in the -- not in the lower court but in the court in which he appealed.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Absolutely not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No? Suppose the state had tried out -- started out from the felony charge?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then indicted at the Superior Court. Now, they would?ve tried him with a felony and they could have found him guilty in the lesser event.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So they would -- could?ve tried him for both of them.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: No, they could?ve tried him on the felony and found him guilty of the misdemeanor. They could not, in effect, have two successes of trials.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He could not have been convicted, I gather, on the felony indictment in both of the felony and the misdemeanor.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He could?ve been convicted of the felony or the jury could?ve convicted him of the misdemeanor.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That?s correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They tried him on the felony and the jury convicted him with the misdemeanor. Certainly then, he could not be tried again for felony. Like in Green.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That?s correct, right. But we contend that the state was not free, having made their election in the first instance, to then reverse course and attempt to try him with the felony in the Superior Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You?re saying, in effect, that they?d done the same -- attempted the same thing as they went through within Green.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Right. And, the major point I would make is that we would contend that if Perry had not appealed, for instance he had been given the six-month sentence in the District Court, if he had said ?fine, that?s a justsentence and I?ll take my punishment and go off to jail,? we certainly contend at that point the state would not have been free to turn around and say ?well, you?re going up to Superior Court in this felony charge.?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well I gather your brother agrees, at least under North Carolina law, they could not have tried him on the felony charge had he not appealed the misdemeanor conviction. As I understood his argument, it?s that they could try him on the felony charge because of his appeal from the misdemeanor conviction reopened.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: I know of no other way they could?ve re-tried it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What is the North Carolina -- is it a constitutional rule or a statutory rule, or a rule of practice, or what? If you assume he had to appeal from his misdemeanor conviction.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Well, North Carolina has basically a rule of practice, but I would also pretend it?s a constitutional rule.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But actually, what powers are articulated in North Carolina, just as a rule of practice?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes, but I mean it?s not articulated in a written form.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: As long as the misdemeanor conviction stands, they just don?t charge him the felony.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Right. Because of the felony, they could try it for that in the first instance and they?re not forced to effect trial for the lesser included offense and then go on and try him for the felony later. They have the option at the first instance of proceeding with the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That?s their practice?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: It was proceeding with the felony.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you also submit that that?s -- that practice is required by the United States Constitution.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes, that?s exactly what we submit. We submit that when the sate has made an election, they?re bound by that election. That they are not free then to proceed and try to elevate the charge at a de novo proceeding in the Superior Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And as my brother Brennan suggested, I understood your opponent here to agree with you if the case were that the original misdemeanor conviction had been unappealed, had been undisturbed. I think the representative of the Attorney General of your state says ?yes, in that case, we would not,? and I understood him to say ?we could not then bring a felony prosecution for precisely the same event.?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That?s our position.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the only question is whether that rule is different or this appears when, at the defendant?s behest, the original conviction was set aside.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Right, and we contend that a price cannot put on appeal that one must face a more elevated form merely for exercising the right to get that appeal. I want to touch on --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that?s a different point. That?s not a double jeopardy point.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Right, it?s a due process point but I think that the due process and double jeopardy at this point begins to merge.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what you?re saying is it?s a burden on appeal.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: There?s no question about that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Slightly defeated or attempt to the state to hear by then bringing in a felony charge.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Is there anything in the record to show what happened on the trial de novo on the misdemeanor?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Is there anything in the record to show what happened? This man said ?I?m appealing.?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Is there anything on the record to show what happened there?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: In the trial de novo in the misdemeanor?

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: There was no trial de novo.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, what happened to it? It just disappeared.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: It disappeared in to the felony indictment. That?s what happened to it. He was never brought to Superior Court on the misdemeanor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What was the maximum sentence if he was subject to a misdemeanor?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Two years. Under North Carolina law virtue --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What did he get on the felony?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: He got five to seven years concurrent with the sentence being served, which effectively raised the sentence by one year, five days and, one month.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: In your de novo appeal to the Superior Court on your misdemeanor, I take it, it?s then up to the state to reinstitute to prosecution all over again in the Superior Court?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Isn?t the question of a hearing on the record of the defendant, having to take the initiative to bring the case to the court?s attention?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Well, that is correct. The Solicitor sets the matter down for trial on the award generally, the misdemeanor award. And the case is heard all over. The judgment is stricken, in effect, and the defendant is given a jury trial in this instance which you cannot waive. I would like to make one brief point regarding the jury trial. In order to get a jury trial which he was constitutionally entitled to, because the matter of which he was being tried carried a maximum sentence of two years, he had to first, submit to a non-jury trial in District Court because there is no provision under North Carolina law for a jury trial in the District Court. We contend that this was unconstitutional and that it deprived him of a right of a jury trial for two reasons. First, this Court in Ward versus Village of Monroeville that there is no authority for the proposition that a constitutional right can be deferred on the ground that it is available at a subsequent de novo proceeding.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Before you -- excuse me, I apologize for interrupting you but I?m curious. Does the record show or do you know why, after having pleaded not guilty to a misdemeanor, he then pleaded guilty to a felony?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: The record doesn?t show because there was no hearing held in District Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you told us that at the earlier stage, the first trial, the man who was cut would not testify --

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: And the man would not testify at the second trial either.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why did he plead guilty?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: He pled guilty, and I?m basing it on conversations with him and his attorney. Again, there?s been no evidentiary hearing. He pled guilty because, all of a sudden, on this charge which carried a maximum sentence of two years. He was faced with a 10-yaer felony which we contend he could not even constitutionally be placed at jeopardy of. And was, in effect, offered a deal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh! It was a plead bargain.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Which he was told that he would get a concurrent-type sentence if he pled guilty.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: So, in effect, he ran. If I can come back to the jury trial issue for just a moment, in Colten this Court held that a sentence on trial de novo could be increased in a two-tier court system such as that held in North California. We contend, under this decision and under the decision of United States versus Jackson, this creates a dilemma in that a defendant who has to appeal to get his jury trial right because this was a two-year misdemeanor has to run the risk of an increased punishment. And we contend this is precisely what the court said in Jackson cannot be the case with the exercise of a right to a jury trial. He --

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, but he was never sentenced as a result of the District Court?s judgment.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes, he was. He was sentenced to six months. He appealed.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: And then that vacated the thing without more.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That?s correct, but he was placed in jeopardy of receiving after two years in prison in the District Court without his constitutional right to a jury trial. That?s our point.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: But he was never charged in the Superior Court.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: He was charged with a felony --

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Yes, but not with the crime he was charged with in District Court.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: No, he was charged with the greater included offense of what he was tried in the District Court.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: It says as a separate offense.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: No, it?s -- I don?t think there?s any question but it?s a greater included offense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Greater included, what?s that?

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: It?s a novel term, ?a greater included offense.? The state says the elements of the crime tried in the Superior Court were different than the elements of the crime tried in the District Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And they were, weren?t they?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: They include two additional elements.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which you didn?t have to prove in the misdemeanor trial.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Which did not have to prove in the misdemeanor trial, that is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And on de novo, misdemeanor trial would not have to prove either?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That is correct also.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But I gather had he got his de novo Superior Court trial -- it is Superior Court, isn?t it, in the state?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He ran the risk of getting his sentence increased if found guilty by the jury from six months to two years.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Whereas, what in fact happened to him instead of -- he could?ve got up not more than two years instead of which he gets five years and seven months.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Well, what could?ve happened on the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, five to seven years?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Right, although it was concurrent. Nevertheless, it did effectively raise by a year-and-a half the sentence received in the District Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does the state have the option of starting a misdemeanor trial for you?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: No, it does not. It must try a misdemeanor in the first instance in the District Court. The statute is cited in my brief and, on that score, the state has to proceed with the first instance to a District Court on a misdemeanor. On a felony, it can proceed directly to the Superior Court or it could proceed to the District Court for preliminary hearing and then on to Superior Court for trial. A misdemeanor could be tried twice: first, in the District Court and, secondly, in the Superior Court de novo.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: When was this code -- when was this codified in your state?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: This has been the procedure for years. I really can?t give you the date, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: 100 years or so?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: 100 years or more?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: I would think so. Yes, sir. It?s an old established procedure in the State of North Carolina. Of course, we?ve got a problem in that Mr. Perry pled guilty in the Superior Court to the felony. And I realize the burden to distinguish that from the Brady trilogy and from Tollett versus Henderson. We contend that there are several tailing distinctions. In the first place, and of course this I?ll state. This is in effect, taking as granted the validity of the double jeopardy argument of which the whole argument is based. The first argument is in that each of those prior cases there was a legitimate state interest in punishing the person for the crime to which the guilty plea was entered. We disagree with Mr. League that the Double Jeopardy Clause was designed just to protect the innocent. I think it?s clear that it is also designed to protect the guilty from repeated punishment and we contend that the state had no legitimate interest, in this particular instance, in punishing Jimmy Seth Perry on a felony of assault with a deadly weapon with intent to kill effecting serious bodily injury. They lost that right when they made a determination to proceed with a misdemeanor in the District Court. Secondly, we contend that on the issues involved in the other cases, there was a situation that existed where if the infirmities involved were corrected, the trial could proceed and punishment could be imposed, for instance in the case of Tollett versus Henderson, illegally constituted grand jury. This is not the Tollett defendant. He is free to go.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What would you say if the defendant says to his lawyer ?well, could I get tried with felony?? And he says ?well, that?s an unsettled issue. It?s about 50-50 I would guess in the Supreme Court. I don?t know whether you?d win or not but, now, we got to make a choice.? Now, the prosecutor says ?we?ll -- we could have a deal on two years if you plead guilty.? Now, it may be that you shouldn?t plead guilty at all. We could win on double jeopardy. The prosecutor doesn?t think its double jeopardy. I think it is, but I don?t know whether I am right.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: In that particular instance, where there is a conscious discussion of the fact that the issue was there and that it can be presented or not presented based on tactical considerations. I think that it could probably be held to be a surrender of double jeopardy to plead guilty. But, what I?m saying is that an issue such as double jeopardy that factor has to be there.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, neither Tollett nor McMann certainly spoke in terms of conscious decision and consultation. They said that once you have the guilty plea, that?s the end of it so far as everything that antedated the guilty plea regardless of any waiver type test like having your right of counsel cases.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Right, that is correct. But what we?re alleging is, in those particular instances the fact that the person pled guilty was significant because of the fact that the guilty plea, in effect, resolved the factual matters at issue in the case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He doesn?t have a guilty plea but it was based on in or after adequate representation by counsel?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes. In this particular instance, the fact that Jimmy Seth Perry may in fact have been guilty of a felony, we contend, is just totally irrelevant if our double jeopardy claim is valid. The fact that he may have, in fact, committed the felony would not, in effect, give the state the right to punish him for the felony.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If you were representing a defendant and you were assured of your double jeopardy point, as you are here, you would never permit him to plead guilty.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That is correct. I would not have pleaded Mr. Perry guilty.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, still in Brady and plus the other cases suggest that perhaps these cases are the ones with guilty please and then you turn it to a representation of counsel case.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think that in the view of the state and the law that the attorney representing Mr. Perry was -- furnished inadequate representations by permitting him to plead guilty?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: I am in this instance, speaking outside the record because I?ve had frequent discussions with the attorney recently regarding this. At the time, the attorney had no comprehension that such an issue existed. He did not discuss it with Mr. Perry. He thought, in terms of the fact, that it was trial de novo and, therefore, the state was free to do what they pleased.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It?s hard for you to find the case that would also support your view, isn?t it?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: I don?t think so. I think Green and Pryce support me. I don?t think the double jeopardy question is that hard to see. I think, in fact, it?s a clear double jeopardy. I think that the attorney should have been aware of the fact that it did exist.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: You speak rather readily of what you would do, but what if the defendant said ?I insist on pleading guilty,? and it?s quite clear that he has that right, isn?t it?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes, I think a person has a right to waive double jeopardy so long as he knows he?s waiving it. I don?t have any problem with that. If he makes an intelligent and knowing decision that, ?yes, it?s in my best interest for tactical reasons to,? let?s say ?plead guilty even though I may have been --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Would you say that if the concept of double jeopardy were absolute bar to a second prosecution where it applies?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: There might be a situation, Mr. Justice Brennan, where a person, let?s say, is charged with 10 felonies --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: What is the concept of double jeopardy? Is it supposed to be an absolute bar to a later prosecution?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That?s the way --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: In any situation where it applies?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: That?s what I believe it to be.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, then I must confess. I?m surprised to hear you say it can be waived.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: I would say it only could be waived -- only in the first instance where a person knows in fact what he?s doing. I could conceive of tactical situations where it might be waived.

turns.name: HARRY BLACKMUN
turns.text: The exclusionary rule of Mapp against Ohio is an absolute bar to the introduction of evidence wrongfully seized. Certainly, that would be waived, isn?t it?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: That?s quite different from double jeopardy. I didn?t suggest it was the same right as the double jeopardy.[Laughter]

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: One is constitutional and one isn?t.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: One other point we wish to make is that, with regards to double jeopardy, the law on which Mr. Perry primarily was basing his claim in the District Court, Wood versus Ross a Fourth Circuit decision, postdated the entering of his guilty plea. We contend that the fact that double jeopardy has been given retroactive effect is significant on this particular score. I realize, in prior cases this Court has held that when a guilty plea is made, it?s not set aside merely because later developments of the law may in fact give a person a valid claim. But again, we would go back to our position that in these particular instances the state had a legitimate interest in punishing the person for the charge involved. In this particular instance, there is no legitimate interest. And secondly, to perhaps join the point Mr. Justice Brennan just briefly made, this is an instance where if the person knew of the double jeopardy bar particularly in the facts of this particular case, there would?ve been no reason in the world to waive it.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, there was no legitimate interest if there was double jeopardy.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: I?ll grant you, I?ve got to convince this Court there was a double jeopardy violation or denial of a right to a jury trial, one or the other. I can?t prevail there was no constitutional deprivation. I?ll concede that.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: What?s the state suppose to do when the double jeopardy question is unsettled?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Trial or not?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: I think the state has the right to proceed the trial.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: It has an interest, doesn?t it?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes, it does. But, the state does -- if the double jeopardy issue should be cited against the state, then the state?s interest is forfeited or lost.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: That?s what the question is about, to plead guilty.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And that?s against the background (Inaudible).

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: The state does have an interest. But the question of whether or not the guilty plea surrenders or forfeits the double jeopardy claim, we think, in the question of double jeopardy which we do see as an absolute bar to re-trial brings about fact that the state must, in fact, show that the person consciously made a decision to surrender the right. The second conceivable situation would be where a person deliberately bypassed state remedies. Again, this would be a very, very rare case because why would a defendant forfeit a possible state remedy? The other question that was brief related to what Boykin requires that a defendant be warned of. If the rule is to be a flat rule that one who pleads guilty surrenders any prior constitutional right with no qualifications whatsoever, we would contend that the defendant ought to be told that at the time he pleads guilty so that here could be no question in his mind that this is not a matter that?s going to be resolved through later writs or through later court proceedings. In this particular instance the judge did run through a series formal of questions, which are in the record, with the defendant and which indicated that he was warned that he could receive a certain maximum sentence, and that he did have a right to trial by jury, and that he did have a right to be represented by counsel and so forth. However, there is nothing in the record that indicates and in effect of what?s been asked. There?s nothing that indicates that Mr. Perry was informed. If you plead guilty here, don?t ever come back to court in any constitutional claim. And we contend that if that is to be the rule, then the defendant should be told precisely that.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Did Judge Larkins rule on the Boykin type claim or did he just not reach it?

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: He just didn?t reach it, Mr. Justice Rehnquist. He held that there was a violation of double jeopardy, one, and secondly, that it wasn?t waived by the plea of guilty. Thank you.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Mr. League, do you have anything further?

turns.name: RICHARD N. LEAGUE
turns.text: No, Your Honor. Thank you.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Keenan, you appeared here at our request by the appointment of this Court.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: On behalf of the Court, thank you for your assistance to your client and your assistance to the Court.

turns.name: JAMES E. KEENAN
turns.text: My pleasure, sir.
